NOTICE MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS ON ELECTRICAL INDUSTRY SERVICE BUREAUS FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT (TRANSACTION ID # 62607665) FILED BY DEFENDANT THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY'S MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS ON THE SOLE REMAINING CAUSE OF ACTION FOR VIOLATION OF THE UNFAIR COMPETITION LAW IN THE FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT FILED BY PLAINTIFF ELECTRICAL INDUSTRY SERVICE BUREAU, INC. IS GRANTED WITHOUT LEAVE TO AMEND. EISB HAS NOT ALLEGED ANY FACTS NOR DOES IT APPEAR THAT IT CAN ALLEGE ANY FACTS ENTITLING IT TO RESTITUTION, THE ONLY MONETARY RELIEF AVAILABLE ON A UCL CLAIM. EISB HAS NOT ALLEGED, NOR DOES IT APPEAR THAT IT IS ABLE TO ALLEGE, THAT IT PROVIDED ANYTHING OF VALUE TO TRAVELERS. THE CASE LAW CITED BY EISB IS INAPPLICABLE BECAUSE THE PLAINTIFFS IN THOSE CASES CONFERRED SOME FORM OF BENEFIT ON THE DEFENDANTS FOR WHICH PLAINTIFFS WERE NEVER COMPENSATED. MOREOVER, MORADI-SHALAL V. FIREMAN'S FUND INSURANCE COMPANIES (1988) 46 CAL.3D 287, 292 PRECLUDES A UCL CLAIM BASED ON INSURANCE CODE 790.03. FORMAL ORDER SIGNED. DEFENDANT MAY SUBMIT A PROPOSED JUDGMENT TO THE COURT FOR SIGNATURE. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN, CLERK: SEAN KANE, COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR#8600, MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM, REPORTED. (302/HK)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jan-03-2019 Continued To Mar-13-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings On Electrical Industry Service Bureaus First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Nov-28-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. TANISHA M. ARATA, ESQ. of Neyhart, Anderson, Flynn & Grosboll (Tel: 415-677-9440) appearing for plaintiff Electrical Industry Service Bureau, Inc.;
Atty. LIAM K. MALONE, ESQ. of Oles Morrison Rinker Baker LLP (Tel: 510-903-2013) appearing for defendant Travelers Indemnity Company.

Matter is argued and submitted by counsel for parties. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company's motion for judgment on the pleadings on the sole remaining cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Electrical Industry Service Bureau, Inc. is granted without leave to amend. EISB has not alleged any facts nor does it appear that it can allege any facts entitling it to restitution, the only monetary relief available on a UCL claim. EISB has not alleged, nor does it appear that it is able to allege, that it provided anything of value to Travelers. The case law cited by EISB is inapplicable because the plaintiffs in those cases conferred some form of benefit on the defendants for which plaintiffs were never compensated. Moreover, Moradi-Shalal v. Fireman's Fund Insurance Companies (1988) 46 Cal.3d 287, 292 precludes a UCL claim based on Insurance Code 790.03.

FORMAL ORDER SIGNED. Defendant may submit a proposed judgment to the Court for signature.

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: Sean Kane, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/HK)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Nov-05-2018 Continued To Jan-16-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-01-2018 Continued To Nov-21-18 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Sep-24-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Tanisha Arata, Esq. 415-677-9440 of Neyhart, Anderson, Flynn & Grossol for Plaintiff.
Liam Malone, Esq. 510-903-2013 of Oles Morrison for Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company'S Demurrer To 1st Amended Complaint.
Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company's demurrer to both causes of action alleged in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Electrical Industry Service Bureau, Inc. is sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for failure to pay on a payment bond and overruled as to the second cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law. The prior tentative ruling overruling the demurrer to the second cause of action was confirmed at the September 4, 2108 hearing. At that hearing the demurrer to the first cause of action was continued to allow the parties to provide further briefing on equitable tolling. Review of that further briefing shows that EISB's equitable tolling argument lacks merit as a matter of law. EISB filed this lawsuit after the expiration of the limitations period for a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond. EISB's effort to be relieved of its untimely first cause of action based on equitable tolling is unavailing. EISB has not alleged that it filed a "claim" prior to filing of this lawsuit. Thus, for instance, in Structural Steel Fabricators, Inc. v. City of Orange (1995) 40 Cal. App. 4th 449, the "first claim" was a prior lawsuit. Nor does McDonald v. Antelope Valley Community College District (2008) 45 Cal. 4th 88 support EISB's equitable tolling argument. The internal administrative remedy involved in McDonald had adjudicative qualities to it unlike EISB's submission of a claim to Travelers. Moreover, federal case law cited in Travelers' supplemental memorandum shows that the federal courts that have considered the same or substantially the same equitable tolling argument made by EISB have rejected that argument. In the absence of any controlling California authority, this court finds that federal case law persuasive. Liberally construed, the allegations in the FAC suggest that EISB may be seeking to invoke equitable estoppel as a basis to avoid the statute of limitations governing a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond, notwithstanding that EISB does not mention equitable estoppel in either the FAC or in its opposition memorandum. Nonetheless, EISB's allegations fall far short of what is required to adequately plead equitable estoppel. (Lantzy v. Centex Homes (2003) 31 Cal. 4th 363, 385). Citing Krantz v. BT Visual Images, L.L.C. (2001) 89 Cal. App. 4th 164, Travelers's sole argument in its initial memorandum as to the UCL claim is that the UCL claim fails because the payment bond claim failed. Not so. Unlike this case, Krantz did not involve the failure of an "antecedent claim" based on the statute of limitations. Per Business and Professions Code 17208, the UCL has its own statute of limitations and that four year limitations period, not the limitations period of the "antecedent" claim, generally governs the UCL claim. (Cortez v. Purolator Air Filtration Products Co. (2000) 23 Cal 4th 163, 178-79 (four year limitations period applies to UCL claim based on a violation of the Labor Code even though the Labor Code had a shorter limitations period)); see also Business and Professions Code 17205 (UCL claims are cumulative of claims under other California laws)). Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M.Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, torreanom@aol.com, Reported. =(302/HK)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-30-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Continued to September 4, 2018 at 9:30 am in Dept. 302. Due to the unavailability of counsel for EISB on August 30, if the tentative ruling is contested, the hearing on this demurrer will be on September 4, 2018 or such other day as counsel for both sides agree. Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company's demurrer to both causes of action alleged in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Electrical Industry Service Bureau, Inc. is sustained without leave to amend as to the first cause of action for failure to pay on a payment bond and overruled as to the second cause of action for violation of the Unfair Competition Law. EISB filed this lawsuit after the expiration of the limitations period for a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond. EISB's effort to be relieved of its untimely first cause of action based on equitable tolling is unavailing. EISB has not alleged that it filed a "claim" prior to filing of this lawsuit. Thus, for instance, in Structural Steel Fabricators, Inc. v. City of Orange (1995) 40 Cal. App. 4th 449, the "first claim" was a prior lawsuit. Liberally construed, the allegations in the FAC suggest that EISB may be seeking to invoke equitable estoppel as a basis to avoid the statute of limitations governing a claim for failure to pay on a payment bond, notwithstanding that EISB does not mention equitable estoppel in either the FAC or in its opposition memorandum. Nonetheless, EISB's allegations fall far short of what is required to adequately plead equitable estoppel. (Lantzy v. Centex Homes (2003) 31 Cal. 4th 363, 385). Citing Krantz v. BT Visual Images, L.L.C. (2001) 89 Cal. App. 4th 164, Travelers's sole argument in its initial memorandum as to the UCL claim is that the UCL claim fails because the payment bond claim failed. Not so. Unlike this case, Krantz did not involve the failure of an "antecedent claim" based on the statute of limitations. Per Business and Professions Code 17208, the UCL has its own statute of limitations and that four year limitations period, not the limitations period of the "antecedent" claim, generally governs the UCL claim. (Cortez v. Purolator Air Filtration Products Co. (2000) 23 Cal 4th 163, 178-79 (four year limitations period applies to UCL claim based on a violation of the Labor Code even though the Labor Code had a shorter limitations period)); see also Business and Professions Code 17205 (UCL claims are cumulative of claims under other California laws)).

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M.Goodman, Court Reporter: Brenda Crow, CSR#10503, bcrow@sftc.org, Reported. =(302/HK)
